title: The conductor who remembers stops
summary: On a small Swiss train, the conductor knows where every regular passenger gets off — and quietly waits for the ones who do not arrive.
body:
Mr Emil works on a small train in Switzerland. The train goes between two mountain towns. The trip takes one hour.

Emil is the conductor. He checks the tickets. He opens the doors. He says hello and goodbye.

The train has the same passengers every day. A teacher gets on at the third station. A boy gets on at the fifth. An old woman gets on at the seventh and gets off at the ninth.

Emil knows every stop. He does not ask. He looks at a person, and he knows.

'Good morning, Frau Berger,' he says. 'Your stop is in ten minutes.' She smiles. She does not need to look at the window.

One winter morning, the old woman did not get on. Emil waited. The doors stayed open one minute longer. The driver did not say anything. The driver knew.

The next day, the old woman did not get on either. Or the day after.

A week later, a young man got on at the seventh station. He looked like the old woman. He had her eyes.

'Are you the son of Frau Berger?' Emil asked.

'Yes,' the man said. 'My mother is in the hospital.'

'I am sorry,' Emil said. 'Her stop is at the ninth station. Please tell her the train still passes there.'

The man was quiet. Then he said, 'I will tell her. She will like that.'

The train left the station. The doors closed. Emil walked to the next car.

annotations.meaningVi: nhân viên soát vé / phụ trách trên tàu
bước lên / lên tàu (phrasal verb: get on)
bước xuống / xuống tàu (phrasal verb: get off)
toa kế tiếp (xe lửa: 'car' = toa tàu)

grammarNotes.title: Phrasal verbs with 'get'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong bài có cặp phrasal verb rất hay đi với phương tiện công cộng: 'get on' = lên (tàu, xe buýt), 'get off' = xuống. 'A teacher gets on at the third station.' 'An old woman gets off at the ninth.' Ở quá khứ: 'got on', 'got off'. Lưu ý phrasal verb không cùng nghĩa với từng từ rời.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The passage uses two key phrasal verbs for public transport: 'get on' = board, 'get off' = leave (a train/bus). 'A teacher gets on at the third station.' 'An old woman gets off at the ninth.' Past forms: 'got on', 'got off'. The verb + particle has its own meaning, not the sum of parts.

translationVi:
Ông Emil làm việc trên một đoàn tàu nhỏ ở Thụy Sĩ. Tàu chạy giữa hai thị trấn vùng núi. Hành trình mất một giờ.

Emil là nhân viên soát vé. Ông kiểm tra vé. Ông mở cửa. Ông chào và tạm biệt.

Đoàn tàu có cùng những hành khách mỗi ngày. Một cô giáo lên ở ga thứ ba. Một cậu bé lên ở ga thứ năm. Một bà cụ lên ở ga thứ bảy và xuống ở ga thứ chín.

Emil biết từng điểm xuống. Ông không cần hỏi. Ông nhìn một người là biết.

'Chào bà Berger,' ông nói. 'Mười phút nữa là tới bến của bà.' Bà cụ mỉm cười. Bà không cần nhìn ra cửa sổ.

Một buổi sáng mùa đông, bà cụ không lên tàu. Emil chờ. Cửa mở lâu hơn một phút. Bác lái tàu không nói gì. Bác lái biết.

Hôm sau, bà cụ cũng không lên. Hôm sau nữa cũng vậy.

Một tuần sau, một người đàn ông trẻ lên ở ga thứ bảy. Anh ta trông giống bà cụ. Anh có đôi mắt giống bà.

'Anh là con trai của bà Berger phải không?' Emil hỏi.

'Vâng,' người đàn ông đáp. 'Mẹ tôi đang ở bệnh viện.'

'Tôi rất tiếc,' Emil nói. 'Bến của mẹ anh là ga thứ chín. Xin nói với mẹ anh rằng tàu vẫn đi qua đó.'

Người đàn ông im lặng. Rồi anh nói: 'Tôi sẽ nói. Mẹ tôi sẽ thích nghe điều đó.'

Tàu rời ga. Cửa đóng. Emil đi sang toa kế tiếp.

options: Between two mountain towns in Switzerland
questions.explanation: 'The train goes between two mountain towns.'

questions.prompt: How does Emil know each passenger's stop?
options: He looks at the person and knows — he does not ask
questions.explanation: 'He does not ask. He looks at a person, and he knows.'

questions.prompt: At which station does Frau Berger get off?
questions.explanation: 'An old woman gets on at the seventh and gets off at the ninth.'

options: Frau Berger did not get on the train
questions.explanation: 'One winter morning, the old woman did not get on.'

questions.prompt: What did the driver do that morning?
options: Kept the doors open one minute longer without saying anything
questions.explanation: 'The doors stayed open one minute longer. The driver did not say anything. The driver knew.'
questions.hint: Just after she did not arrive.

questions.prompt: Who got on the train a week later?
questions.explanation: 'A young man got on at the seventh station. He looked like the old woman.'

questions.prompt: What did Emil ask the son to tell his mother?
options: That the train still passes the ninth station
That he hopes she gets well soon
That her ticket is still valid
That a seat is reserved for her
questions.explanation: 'Please tell her the train still passes there.'

questions.prompt: Why does that message matter to her?
options: Knowing that a familiar routine is still happening is comforting when one is sick
She wants to buy more tickets
She works for the train company
She is angry about the trip
questions.explanation: Inference: the son says she will 'like that.'
questions.hint: Think about why the message would help her.

options: Quiet attention to regular people is a kind of care that does not need to announce itself
Conductors should know every passenger's address
Mountain towns are hard to reach
questions.hint: Think about what Emil does without being asked.

questions.prompt: What does 'either' mean in 'did not get on either'?

blanks.explanation: 'He looks at a person, and he knows.'
She does not need to look at the window.
His mother is in the hospital.
Walked to the next car (train carriage).

criticalThinkingQuestion: Emil asks the son to tell his sick mother 'the train still passes there.' Why might that small fact be a comfort? Is there a small, ordinary fact about your own daily route — a street, a tree, a market stall — that you would want to know was still there if you could not visit it?
